items.title: Amateur armed with ChatGPT solves an Erdős problem
items.author: pr337h4m
items.summary: An amateur used an AI system to solve a 60-year-old math problem. The achievement was notable due to the solver's lack of advanced training. The AI system was built by a team of inventors at Amazon.

items.title: USB Cheat Sheet (2022)
items.author: gwerbret
items.summary: USB supports data modes from USB1.1 to USB4 v2, with charging speeds ranging from 7.5W to 240W. USB connectors include USB-A, and it uses pins like Vbus, GND, D+, D-, and CC1. USB modes have specific marketing names and technical definitions.

items.title: GnuPG – post-quantum crypto landing in mainline
items.author: zdkaster
items.summary: GnuPG has integrated post-quantum cryptography into its infrastructure, preparing for quantum computer threats. Post-quantum keys are now supported and distributed via Web Key Directory. This update ensures future-proof encryption.

items.title: OpenAI Privacy Filter
items.author: tanelpoder
items.summary: The OpenAI Privacy Filter is a model for detecting and redacting personally identifiable information in text. It is available under the Apache 2.0 license on Hugging Face and GitHub. It has limitations in detecting uncommon names and over-redacting public entities.

items.title: 1-Bit Hokusai's "The Great Wave" (2023)
items.author: stephen-hill
items.summary: In 2023, a 1-bit pixel art version of Hokusai's "The Great Wave" was created, capturing the original artwork in black and white. This project aimed to recreate the woodcut in the resolution of early Macintosh computers. The work was featured on Hacker News and Hypertalking.

items.title: America's Geothermal Breakthrough
items.author: sleepyguy
items.summary: America achieved a significant breakthrough in geothermal energy, reducing costs and improving efficiency, led by the University of Utah's FORGE project. Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS) are now more viable. This innovation promises sustainable heating and cooling solutions.

items.title: New 10 GbE USB adapters are cooler, smaller, cheaper
items.author: calcifer
items.summary: New 10 GbE USB adapters are now available, offering improved cooling, reduced size, and lower cost compared to previous models. These adapters use RTL8159 technology and are priced around $80. They provide a more efficient alternative to traditional solutions.

items.title: Per-image PCA characterization of the Kodak image suite (PDF and JSON)
items.author: PearsonZero
items.summary: Per-image PCA characterization of the Kodak image suite reveals unique statistical properties for each image, highlighting deliberate curation and differences in color predictability. The analysis is available on GitHub.

items.title: Math Is Hard – OpenBSD Stories
items.author: signa11
items.summary: "Math Is Hard" is a series of stories about OpenBSD on Motorola 88K by Miod Vallat. It includes a 9-part narrative and a single-page version. The series is popular on Hacker News, X, and Reddit.

items.title: Hokusai and Tesselations
items.author: srean
items.summary: Hokusai's tessellations merge traditional Japanese art with modern data visualization, notably Bitcoin charts. They use precise geometric patterns inspired by Hokusai's waves. These tessellations are highly valued for their artistic and technical significance.

items.title: DeepSeek-V4 on Day 0: From Fast Inference to Verified RL with SGLang and Miles
items.author: mji
items.summary: DeepSeek-V4 launched with fast inference and verified RL training using SGLang and Miles. It supports multiple hardware configurations, including RTX PRO 6000. The release was celebrated on social media.

items.title: The George Business, by Roger Zelazny (1980)
items.author: xeonmc
items.summary: "The George Business" is a short story by Roger Zelazny, published in 1980. It is part of his speculative fiction works. The story is included in various collections and lists of his works.

items.title: GPT‑5.5 Bio Bug Bounty
items.author: Murfalo
items.summary: GPT-5.5 Bio Bug Bounty by OpenAI aims to improve AI safety against biological risks. It invites researchers to identify vulnerabilities in its latest model. Participation is restricted to ensure responsible disclosure.

items.title: Colorado Adds Open-Source Exemption to Age-Verification Bill
items.author: terminalbraid
items.summary: Colorado's age-verification bill now exempts open-source operating systems, applications, and code repositories. This exemption aims to protect the open-source community. The bill focuses on operating system providers and application stores.

items.title: Plain text has been around for decades and it’s here to stay
items.author: rbanffy
items.summary: Plain text has been a standard format for decades and remains widely used today. Its simplicity and compatibility make it enduring. It continues to be a preferred choice for many applications.

items.title: Her life savings mysteriously disappeared after a systems glitch
items.author: danso
items.summary: A systems glitch caused a woman's life savings to disappear, with her accounts showing zero dollars. Fidelity Investments informed her that her phone number and email were removed from her profile. The incident highlights risks in digital financial systems.

items.title: Which one is more important: more parameters or more computation? (2021)
items.author: jxmorris12
items.summary: More computation often leads to better performance, but fewer parameters can achieve efficiency and avoid overfitting. The balance between parameters and computation depends on the specific task and dataset. Ultimately, the most effective approach varies by context.

items.title: Insights into firewood use by early Middle Pleistocene hominins
items.author: wslh
items.summary: Early Middle Pleistocene hominins at Gesher Benot Ya'aqov collected driftwood for fire, including ash, willow, olive, pistachio, and pomegranate, primarily based on availability. The study confirms they used any available wood, showing a diverse range of habitats and resources. This behavior highlights their adaptability and resourcefulness.

items.title: Only one side will be the true successor to MS-DOS – Windows 2.x
items.author: keepamovin
items.summary: Windows 2.x was not the true successor to MS-DOS; OS/2 1.x ultimately succeeded MS-DOS. Windows 2.x was a shell program, but it failed to replace DOS. OS/2 1.x provided a more successful GUI alternative.

items.title: Iliad fragment found in Roman-era mummy
items.author: wise_blood
items.summary: A fragment of Homer's Iliad was found inside a Roman-era mummy's abdomen, indicating unique burial practices. The discovery was made by a Spanish-Egyptian team in Egypt's Minya Governorate. This is the first time a Greek literary text was found incorporated into the preservation process.
